EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early January cold front pushing into southern Utah had a favorable orientation for a period of enhanced gap winds. This was particularly valid for southwest Utah where strong winds were noted, including at least one instance of a gust to 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to strong winds throughout the zone, a trained spotter reported a wind gust to 60 mph recorded on a personal weather station early in the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure set up across eastern Utah overnight from January 3 to 4, leading to clear skies and light winds. Pools of strong moisture remained within many valleys though from precipitation earlier in January. With this, cooling overnight temperatures led to a saturated near-surface environment and widespread dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog developed within the Uinta Basin overnight from January 3 to 4. The KVEL ASOS at the Vernal Regional Airport reported dense freezing fog persisted for the majority of the night, bringing visibility to 1/4 mile. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure set up across eastern Utah overnight from January 3 to 4, leading to clear skies and light winds. Pools of strong moisture remained within many valleys though from precipitation earlier in January. With this, cooling overnight temperatures led to a saturated near-surface environment and widespread dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog developed within the Utah Grand Flat overnight from January 3 to 4. The KCNY ASOS at the Canyonlands Regional Airport reported dense fog persisted for the majority of the night, bringing visibility to 1/4 mile or less. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak southwesterly flow around a deepening trough brought mid-level moisture over eastern Utah, prompting snow showers to portions of the region overnight from January 7 to 8. Breaks in cloud cover enabled enough radiational cooling to saturate some locations overnight, and localized dense fog was able to develop as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized dense fog developed on the northern slopes of the Uintah Mountains on the morning of January 8. The K40U AWOS at the Manila Airport reported a brief period of 1/4-mile visibility due to freezing fog. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong moisture transport flowed through the Intermountain West around a deep upper-level trough in late January, bringing widespread precipitation to eastern Utah. While much of the region continued to be impacted by active weather through the night and morning, drying mid-levels cleared skies over portions of the Utah Grand Flat and Four Corners. Mist and fog lowered visibility for much of the night as a result, with dense fog briefly developing coinciding with the clearest skies and strongest radiational cooling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized dense fog developed within the Utah Grand Flat on the morning of January 24. The KCNY ASOS at the Canyonlands Regional Airport reported about one hour of 1/4-mile visibility from dense fog after midnight. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter of 2025-2026 has been, overall, a dry and warm one. And as such, drought has clung on across eastern Utah and western Colorado, especially across the Central Divide Mountains. Areas in the eastern Uinta mountains have also seen drought linger.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of January, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought as covering 25% of Utah Zone 023 - Eastern Uinta Mountains. Severe (D2) Drought covered 70% of the zone, and Moderate (D1) Drought covered the remaining 5%. With the 1/7 Drought Monitor update, the area of Extreme (D3) Drought was removed, with 30% of the zone now under Severe (D2) Drought conditions, and the remaining 70% under Moderate (D1) Drought. No reports of impacts or damages have been received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An open low pressure trough moved through the area with better dynamics and moisture at the onset, with the Park Range Mountains favored for the better accumulations. Moderate snow continued across the central and southern Colorado mountains as well as the La Sal Mountains and Abajo Mountains of southeast Utah, driven mainly from orographic components. Temperatures were fairly mild, which limited snow accumulations to the higher elevations above 8000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 12 inches of snow fell across the La Sal Mountains with 6 to 8 inches of snow across the Abajo Mountains. A peak wind gust of 46 mph occurred at Abajo Peak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A jet streak and shortwave trough pushed through the northern and central portions of the area, bringing moderate snowfall to the northern and central Colorado mountains as well as the Eastern Uinta Mountains of northeast Utah. The strong southwest flow also led to windy conditions at the higher elevations resulting in blowing snow as well. Temperatures stayed warm with this system, keeping better snow accumulations mainly confined to areas above 9000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow ahead of a deepening cutoff low over the Desert Southwest allowed for sufficient moisture to work into the area along a strong cold front. An additional low pressure system dropped in from the northwest, bringing colder air with it and pushing this cold front across the area. This resulted in moderate to heavy snowfall across the central and southern mountains of western Colorado and southeast Utah with the higher accumulations over the Uncompahgre Plateau and San Juan Mountains due to colder air and favorable orographics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 12 inches of snow fell across the higher elevations of the area with 7 to 9 inches of snow over the Abajo Mountains and 9 to 12 inches of snow over the La Sal Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While somewhat mild by January standards, a moisture rich system brought a period of valley rain and mountain snow, with some higher accumulations noted particularly in portions of Utah's northern mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Ben Lomond Peak, Lightning Ridge, and Garden City Summit recorded from 1.5 to 3.2 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 19 to 40 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While somewhat mild by January standards, a moisture rich system brought a period of valley rain and mountain snow, with some higher accumulations noted particularly in portions of Utah's northern mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utah Highway Patrol reported numerous traffic accidents on I-15 between approximately the Nephi exit and roughly two miles northward. The volume of accidents resulted in the southbound lanes of I-15 being closed and traffic being detoured through the city itself. The winter weather is assumed to be the likely cause of the incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While somewhat mild by January standards, a moisture rich system brought a period of valley rain and mountain snow, with some higher accumulations noted particularly in portions of Utah's northern mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement (including dispatch, fire department, and other associated first responders) responded to a report of a collision between a semi truck and passenger vehicle on I-15 southbound near mile marker 384 shortly before 1:30 pm. Thereafter numerous other rollover and collision accidents were reported between mile marker 383 to 387. A total of two individuals were transported for medical attention with minor injuries. Winter weather is assumed to be the cause.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While somewhat mild by January standards, a moisture rich system brought a period of valley rain and mountain snow, with some higher accumulations noted particularly in portions of Utah's northern mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utah Highway Patrol and Utah Department of Transportation reported a multi vehicle crash near mile post 5 on US-40 westbound between Mayflower and Quinns. This accident resulted in a period of road closure for cleanup, and is assumed to be the result of ongoing winter weather.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An open wave low pressure trough moved through Utah and formed a closed low near the Four Corners before pushing through southwest Colorado. Significant moisture ahead of this trough and cold air behind a strong cold front led to moderate to heavy snowfall across the mountains of western Colorado and southeast Utah, with the highest snowfall totals across the Uncompahgre Gorge area of the northwest San Juans due to favorable light northerly flow behind the closed low and temperatures within the dendritic growth zone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area with 6 to 8 inches at both the La Sal Mountains and Abajo Mountains. A peak wind gust of 52 mph occurred at Abajo Peak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep Pacific trough brought moist southwesterly flow to much of the western CONUS on January 1. A weak upper-level jet streak arrived in coincidence with the surge of moisture, prompting widespread precipitation in eastern Utah throughout the morning of the 1st. Strong cold air advection behind the storms rapidly saturated valleys, and areas of dense fog developed during the afternoon and lasting through the morning of January 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog developed near the Abajo mountains during the afternoon and evening of January 1. The KU64 AWOS at the Monticello Airport reported several hours of 1/4-mile visibility due to dense fog. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep Pacific trough brought moist southwesterly flow to much of the western CONUS on January 1. A weak upper-level jet streak arrived in coincidence with the surge of moisture, prompting widespread precipitation in eastern Utah throughout the morning of the 1st. Strong cold air advection behind the storms rapidly saturated valleys, and areas of dense fog developed during the afternoon and lasting through the morning of January 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog developed in the Uinta Basin throughout the morning of January 2. The KVEL ASOS at the Vernal Regional Airport reported multiple periods of reduced visibility due to freezing during the morning. Patchy dense fog developed early in the morning, reducing visibility to 1/4 mile for about an hour before weakening. Visibilities dropped below 1/4 mile after sunrise, lasting about two hours before fog dissipated entirely. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Potent surface moisture lingered within Utah valleys on the morning of January 3 resulting from widespread rain and snow over the prior two days. A deep upper-level ridge moving overhead made way for clear skies though. With the resulting radiational cooling, morning and early afternoon surface conditions became saturated and supported widespread dense fog development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog developed within the Utah Grand Flat on the morning of January 3. The KCNY ASOS at the Canyonlands Regional Airport reported a brief period of 1/4-mile visibility from freezing fog shortly before sunrise. Reduced visibility due to mist and fog remained before a prolonged period of less than 1/4 mile visibility due to dense fog developed a couple hours after sunrise and lasted about three hours. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Potent surface moisture lingered within Utah valleys on the morning of January 3 resulting from widespread rain and snow over the prior two days. A deep upper-level ridge moving overhead made way for clear skies though. With the resulting radiational cooling, morning and early afternoon surface conditions became saturated and supported widespread dense fog development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog developed within the Uinta Basin on the morning of January 3. The KVEL ASOS at the Vernal Regional Airport reported two periods of 1/4 mile visibility or less, one in the morning and one in the afternoon, from freezing fog. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.
